# Shared base for all agent sandbox variants.
# Build with --target claude or --target pi (see README.md / README.pi.md).
#
# Claude: docker build --build-arg BASE_IMAGE=docker/sandbox-templates:claude-code --target claude ...
# Pi:     docker build --build-arg BASE_IMAGE=docker/sandbox-templates:shell       --target pi    ...

# BASE_IMAGE must be declared before the first FROM so it can be used in FROM.
# Override at build time: --build-arg BASE_IMAGE=docker/sandbox-templates:shell
ARG BASE_IMAGE=docker/sandbox-templates:shell

# ── gmux builder ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# Builds gmux + gmuxd from the local fork (injected via --build-context gmuxsrc=).
FROM golang:1.26 AS gmux-builder
# Node.js is required by the vite frontend build step.
RUN curl -fsSL https://deb.nodesource.com/setup_22.x | bash - \
    && apt-get install -y nodejs \
    && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*
COPY --from=gmuxsrc . /gmux
WORKDIR /gmux
# Reinstall node_modules for Linux — host node_modules contain macOS-native
# binaries (e.g. esbuild) that won't run in the builder container.
RUN npm install -g pnpm@10.32.1 \
    && find . -name node_modules -type d -prune -exec rm -rf {} + \
    && pnpm install --frozen-lockfile
RUN bash scripts/build.sh

ARG BASE_IMAGE
FROM ${BASE_IMAGE} AS common

# Switch to root to install system packages and modify system-level config.
# Each target drops back to 'agent' at the end of its stage.
USER root

# Core tools needed by all agent variants.
RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends \
        jq curl unzip python3-pip python3-venv awscli \
    && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*

# Install just (command runner) from the upstream binary release.
RUN curl -fsSL https://just.systems/install.sh | bash -s -- --to /usr/local/bin

# Install Node.js 22.x (LTS) via NodeSource.
# Node 22 adds NODE_COMPILE_CACHE which caches V8 bytecode to disk, giving
# ~3-5x faster cold-start for heavy CJS packages (e.g. @mui/material).
RUN curl -fsSL https://deb.nodesource.com/setup_22.x | bash - \
    && apt-get install -y nodejs \
    && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*

# Install uv (fast Python package manager) system-wide.
# --break-system-packages is required on Ubuntu 25.x which guards the system Python.
RUN pip3 install --break-system-packages uv

# Bake AWS config (SSO profile metadata only — no tokens).
# The file is injected at build time via --build-context awsconfig=~/.aws
# so it never needs to live in this repo.
RUN mkdir -p /home/agent/.aws && chown -R agent:agent /home/agent/.aws

# Copy only the config file (not credentials) from the host's ~/.aws directory.
# --chown ensures agent owns it, so the process can read it without privilege.
COPY --from=awsconfig --chown=agent:agent config /home/agent/.aws/config

# Install gmux binaries built from the local fork.
COPY --from=gmux-builder /gmux/bin/gmux  /usr/local/bin/gmux
COPY --from=gmux-builder /gmux/bin/gmuxd /usr/local/bin/gmuxd

# Strip privileges granted by base image: no passwordless sudo, no docker socket access.
RUN deluser agent sudo 2>/dev/null || true && \
    deluser agent docker 2>/dev/null || true && \
    rm -f /etc/sudoers.d/agent /etc/sudoers.d/90-agent

# Shared SSO token cache: redirect ~/.aws/sso/cache into the workspace mount
# so all sandboxes share the same token and only one device-code auth is needed.
# This snippet is sourced on every bash command by Claude (via CLAUDE_ENV_FILE)
# and on every interactive shell by pi (via ~/.bashrc).
RUN printf '%s\n' \
    '# Shared AWS SSO token cache across all sandboxes' \
    '_sso_shared="/Users/james-carmody/james-agent-workspace/.aws-sso-cache"' \
    '_sso_link="$HOME/.aws/sso/cache"' \
    'if [[ -d "$(dirname "$_sso_shared")" && ! -L "$_sso_link" ]]; then' \
    '  mkdir -p "$_sso_shared" "$HOME/.aws/sso"' \

# sbx — Docker AI Sandboxes CLI

**Docs**: https://docs.docker.com/ai/sandboxes/  
**CLI reference**: https://docs.docker.com/reference/cli/sbx/  
*(Note: for some reason, the claude WebFetch tool returns an empty page when trying to read the cli reference. maybe try and find an llms.txt link or similar in the docker sbx docs)*

## Overview

`sbx` manages isolated microVM sandbox environments for AI agents. Each sandbox gets its own filesystem, network, and Docker daemon. Outbound traffic routes through a host-side proxy with deny-by-default network policy.

## Lifecycle commands

| Command | Description |
|---|---|
| `sbx run AGENT [PATH]` | Create and attach to a sandbox (creates if not exists) |
| `sbx create AGENT PATH` | Create without attaching |
| `sbx stop SANDBOX` | Stop sandbox, preserve state |
| `sbx run SANDBOX` | Resume a stopped sandbox |
| `sbx rm SANDBOX` | Remove sandbox and all state (irreversible) |
| `sbx ls` | List all sandboxes with status, ports, workspace |

**Restart pattern** (no `sbx restart` command exists):
```bash
sbx stop <name> && sbx run <name>
```

## Key flags for `sbx run` / `sbx create`

| Flag | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `--template <image>` | Use a custom container image |
| `--name <name>` | Override default name (`<agent>-<workdir>`) |
| `--branch <branch>` | Create a Git worktree for isolated changes |
| `--kit <ref>` | Apply a kit (YAML config, can be repeated) |
| `--cpus <n>` | CPU allocation (0 = auto) |
| `-m <mem>` | Memory limit e.g. `8g` |

## Running commands inside a sandbox

```bash
sbx exec SANDBOX -- <command>          # run a command
sbx exec -it SANDBOX bash              # interactive shell
sbx exec -u root SANDBOX <command>     # run as root
```

`sbx exec` starts a stopped sandbox automatically before running.

## File transfer

```bash
sbx cp ./file.json SANDBOX:/path/      # host → sandbox
sbx cp SANDBOX:/path/output.log ./     # sandbox → host
```

One side must be `SANDBOX:PATH`, the other a local path. Sandbox-to-sandbox copy not supported.

## Port publishing

Services must bind to `0.0.0.0` (not `127.0.0.1`) inside the sandbox to be reachable via published ports.

### Cloudflare AI Gateway

`CLOUDFLARE_API_KEY` can be set via `/login`. The account ID and gateway slug must be set as environment variables.

Routes to OpenAI, Anthropic, and Workers AI through Cloudflare AI Gateway. Workers AI uses the Unified API (`/compat`) and prefixed model IDs (`workers-ai/@cf/...`). OpenAI uses the OpenAI passthrough route (`/openai`) with native OpenAI model IDs such as `gpt-5.1`. Anthropic uses the Anthropic passthrough route (`/anthropic`) with native Anthropic model IDs such as `claude-sonnet-4-5`.

AI Gateway authentication uses `CLOUDFLARE_API_KEY` as `cf-aig-authorization`. Upstream authentication can be one of:

| Mode | Request auth | Upstream auth |
|------|--------------|---------------|
| Workers AI | Cloudflare token only | Cloudflare-native |
| Unified billing | Cloudflare token only | Cloudflare handles upstream auth and deducts credits |
| Stored BYOK | Cloudflare token only | Cloudflare injects provider keys stored in the AI Gateway dashboard |
| Inline BYOK | Cloudflare token plus upstream `Authorization` header | The request supplies the upstream provider key |

content.text:
Try replacing `aws-vault` temporarily with a logging wrapper. First, find where it's installed:

```bash
which aws-vault
```

Then create a wrapper that logs the caller before passing through:

```bash
REAL=$(which aws-vault)
sudo tee /usr/local/bin/aws-vault-wrapper <<'EOF'
#!/bin/bash
echo "$(date) aws-vault called by PID $PPID ($(ps -o comm= -p $PPID)) args: $@" >> /tmp/aws-vault-calls.log
ps -o pid,ppid,comm -p $PPID >> /tmp/aws-vault-calls.log
echo "---" >> /tmp/aws-vault-calls.log
exec /usr/local/bin/aws-vault.real "$@"
EOF
sudo chmod +x /usr/local/bin/aws-vault-wrapper
```

Or more simply — just use `log stream` with a tighter predicate:

```bash
log stream --predicate 'eventMessage contains "aws-vault" OR process == "aws-vault"' --info
```

Or catch it with a polling loop in a terminal while you wait for the popup:

```bash
while true; do
  pgrep -lx aws-vault && ps -o pid,ppid,command $(pgrep aws-vault) >> /tmp/aws-vault-calls.log
  sleep 1
done
```

The `pgrep` loop is the most reliable given `fs_usage` was noisy. Leave it running and next time the popup fires, `/tmp/aws-vault-calls.log` will have the parent process ID and command that spawned it.

#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail

LOG=/tmp/aws-sso-refresh.log
: > "$LOG"

if [[ "${IS_SANDBOX:-0}" == "1" ]]; then
  PROFILE="scw-agentic-bedrock-sso"
else
  PROFILE="scw-agentic-bedrock"
fi

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Shared SSO token cache: symlink ~/.aws/sso/cache into the workspace so all
# sandboxes (each with their own filesystem) share the same token file.
#
# The workspace is mounted at the same absolute path in every sandbox, so
# once one sandbox authenticates the token is immediately visible to all
# others — they hit the fast-path "session already valid" check below.
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------

SHARED_CACHE_DIR="/Users/james-carmody/james-agent-workspace/.aws-sso-cache"
SSO_CACHE_LINK="$HOME/.aws/sso/cache"

if [[ -d "$(dirname "$SHARED_CACHE_DIR")" ]]; then
  if [[ ! -L "$SSO_CACHE_LINK" ]]; then
    mkdir -p "$SHARED_CACHE_DIR" "$HOME/.aws/sso"
    # Move any existing local tokens into the shared cache before replacing.
    if [[ -d "$SSO_CACHE_LINK" ]]; then
      mv "$SSO_CACHE_LINK"/* "$SHARED_CACHE_DIR"/ 2>/dev/null || true
      rmdir "$SSO_CACHE_LINK" 2>/dev/null || true
    fi
    ln -sfn "$SHARED_CACHE_DIR" "$SSO_CACHE_LINK"
    echo "[aws-sso] SSO cache linked: $SSO_CACHE_LINK -> $SHARED_CACHE_DIR" | tee -a "$LOG"
  fi
else
  echo "[aws-sso] Workspace not mounted at $(dirname "$SHARED_CACHE_DIR"), using local SSO cache" | tee -a "$LOG"
fi

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Cross-sandbox lock: stored in the workspace so all sandboxes coordinate.
# PIDs are not meaningful across VM boundaries, so we use a TTL (90 s).
# A lock older than TTL is considered stale and removed.
# Within a single sandbox, two processes can still race here, but the
# probability is low and the fallback (both try to auth) is safe.
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------

LOCK_DIR="/Users/james-carmody/james-agent-workspace/.aws-sso-refresh.lock"
LOCK_TTL=90  # seconds before a lock is considered stale

acquire_lock() {
  if mkdir "$LOCK_DIR" 2>/dev/null; then
    echo "$(date +%s)" > "$LOCK_DIR/ts"
    echo $$ > "$LOCK_DIR/pid"
    return 0
  fi
  return 1
}

release_lock() {
  [[ -d "$LOCK_DIR" ]] && rm -rf "$LOCK_DIR" || true
}
trap release_lock EXIT

clear_stale_lock() {
  if [[ -d "$LOCK_DIR" ]]; then
    local lock_ts now age
    lock_ts=$(cat "$LOCK_DIR/ts" 2>/dev/null || echo 0)
    now=$(date +%s)
    age=$(( now - lock_ts ))
    if (( age > LOCK_TTL )); then
      echo "[aws-sso] Clearing stale lock (age=${age}s > TTL=${LOCK_TTL}s)" | tee -a "$LOG"
      rm -rf "$LOCK_DIR" 2>/dev/null || true
    fi
  fi
}

clear_stale_lock

if ! acquire_lock; then
  echo "[aws-sso] Waiting for concurrent SSO refresh (up to 120s)..." | tee -a "$LOG"
  waited=0
  while [[ -d "$LOCK_DIR" ]] && (( waited < 120 )); do
    sleep 3
    waited=$(( waited + 3 ))
    # Also clear stale locks while waiting
    clear_stale_lock
  done

  # Check if the concurrent refresh succeeded
  if aws sts get-caller-identity --profile "$PROFILE" > /dev/null 2>&1; then
    echo "[aws-sso] Session valid after concurrent refresh" | tee -a "$LOG"
    exit 0
  fi

  # Session still invalid; try to acquire the lock ourselves
  if ! acquire_lock; then
    echo "[aws-sso] Could not acquire lock after waiting; giving up" | tee -a "$LOG"
    exit 1
  fi
fi

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Fast path: if the session is already valid, nothing to do.
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------

if aws sts get-caller-identity --profile "$PROFILE" > /dev/null 2>&1; then
  echo "[aws-sso] Session already valid, skipping refresh" | tee -a "$LOG"
  exit 0
fi

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Step 1: Silent refresh via OIDC refresh token (no browser, no device code).
#
# For sso-session profiles (which store a refreshToken in ~/.aws/sso/cache/),
# `aws sso login` without --use-device-code will:
#   1. Check for a cached valid access token  → use it
#   2. Check for a valid refresh token        → silently call OIDC CreateToken
#   3. Only fall through to interactive flow  → if both are absent/expired
#
# Using --use-device-code BYPASSES step 2 and always starts a new auth flow,
# which is why re-auth was required every ~1 hour instead of every 8 hours.
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------

echo "[aws-sso] Attempting silent token refresh (OIDC refresh token)..." | tee -a "$LOG"
silent_rc=0
timeout 10 aws sso login --profile "$PROFILE" --no-browser > /tmp/aws-sso-silent.log 2>&1 \
  || silent_rc=$?
cat /tmp/aws-sso-silent.log | tee -a "$LOG"

if [[ $silent_rc -eq 0 ]]; then
  # Verify the session is actually usable now
  if aws sts get-caller-identity --profile "$PROFILE" > /dev/null 2>&1; then
    echo "[aws-sso] Silent refresh succeeded" | tee -a "$LOG"
    exit 0
  fi
fi

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Step 2: Device-code flow (user must open URL in browser).
# Fallback when the OIDC refresh token is absent, expired, or revoked
# (i.e., beyond the sso_session_duration window, typically 8h).
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------

echo "[aws-sso] Silent refresh failed (rc=$silent_rc) — starting device code flow..." | tee -a "$LOG"
# Stream output line-by-line. The moment a URL appears, push it to the
# clipboard via OSC 52 — gmux's xterm.js handler writes it to the system
# clipboard (navigator.clipboard), so it lands in iOS Clipboard without
# the user needing to select/copy text in the browser terminal.
aws sso login --profile "$PROFILE" --no-browser --use-device-code 2>&1 | while IFS= read -r line; do
  printf '%s\n' "$line" | tee -a "$LOG"
  if [[ "$line" =~ (https://[^[:space:]]+) ]]; then
    url="${BASH_REMATCH[1]}"
    b64=$(printf '%s' "$url" | base64 | tr -d '\n')
    printf '\033]52;c;%s\007' "$b64" > /dev/tty
    printf '\n[aws-sso] Auth URL:\n%s\n\n' "$url" | tee -a "$LOG"
  fi
done

#!/bin/bash
# Wait for AWS credentials to be available before starting pi.
# The sandbox network may not be up yet when sbx run first invokes the entrypoint.
# Gives up after 15s and starts pi anyway (expired token errors will surface in pi).
timeout=15
elapsed=0
until aws sts get-caller-identity --profile scw-agentic-bedrock-sso >/dev/null 2>&1; do
  if (( elapsed >= timeout )); then
    echo "pi-launch: AWS credentials not ready after ${timeout}s, starting pi anyway"
    break
  fi
  sleep 1
  elapsed=$(( elapsed + 1 ))
done
exec pi "$@"

```mermaid

    TRYLOCK -- acquired --> FASTPATH
    TRYLOCK -- blocked  --> WAIT["wait up to 120 s\n(poll every 3 s, clear stale lock)"]

    WAIT --> CHECKAFTER{"session valid\nafter wait?"}
    CHECKAFTER -- yes --> E0A([exit 0])
    CHECKAFTER -- no  --> RETRY{"acquire lock\n(second attempt)"}
    RETRY -- fail  --> E1([exit 1 — give up])

On the **host** (IS_SANDBOX ≠ 1), `PROFILE = scw-agentic-bedrock`, which has:

```
credential_process = aws-vault export --format=json scw-agentic-bedrock-sso

Every `aws sts get-caller-identity` call (fast-path check, lock-wait check, validity check in the extension) invokes `aws-vault`, which hits the macOS keychain → **popup**.

This fires at minimum on every **session_start** and every **30-minute poll**, plus whenever Bedrock returns a 401/403.
